note_data.title: Properties of waves
keyPoints: Magnets have north and south poles - like poles repel, opposite poles attract
Magnetic materials (iron, steel, cobalt, nickel) are always attracted to magnets, while non-magnetic materials (glass, plastic) experience no force
Magnetic materials can be magnetised by stroking with a magnet, hammering in a magnetic field, or placing in a coil with direct current
Temporary magnets (soft iron) lose magnetism when the field is removed, permanent magnets retain their magnetism
Electromagnets are temporary magnets made by placing soft iron in coiled wire with direct current
Magnetic field lines show field direction (north to south pole) and strength (closer lines = stronger field)
Electric charge is measured in coulombs and can be positive or negative - opposite charges attract, like charges repel
Conductors allow charge flow through free moving charged particles, insulators impede charge flow
Insulators can be electrostatically charged by friction through transfer of electrons

definitions.definition: A material or object that produces a magnetic field
A region in which a magnetic pole experiences the force from another magnet or magnetic material
Measured in coulombs and can be positive or negative
Materials which allow electric charge to flow through them because they have charged particles that are free to move
Materials which impede the flow of electric charge because they don't have charged particles that are free to move

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Learn exact definitions word-for-word from mark scheme
Show formula, substitution, and answer with units

modelAnswer.question: A wave has a frequency of 50 Hz and travels at a speed of 340 m/s. Calculate the wavelength of the wave.
markBreakdown: Mark 3: Correct answer with unit (6.8 m)

commonMistakes: Thinking magnetic materials are only attracted to specific poles - they are attracted to both poles
Confusing temporary and permanent magnets - temporary magnets lose magnetism when the field is removed
Believing magnetic field lines go from south to north - they go from north to south pole
Thinking only protons move during charging - only electrons transfer during electrostatic charging
examinerTips: Remember magnetic field strength is shown by how close field lines are together - closer lines mean stronger field
When plotting magnetic fields, ensure arrows point from north to south pole on all field lines
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Transverse wave showing amplitude (vertical arrow from rest position to crest), wavelength (horizontal arrow between two crests), and clearly labelled rest position, crest and trough
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (3 marks) — they asked students to determine the phase difference between two points on a wave, with many incorrectly giving 90° instead of 180°
This concept appeared in O/N 2022 P1 Q12 (1 mark) — students had to calculate wave speed using v = fλ, where λ = 0.4 m and frequency = 16 Hz
This was examined in M/J 2024 P2 Q5 (4 marks) — candidates needed to identify compression regions on longitudinal wave displacement graphs, with point B being the correct answer
This appeared in O/N 2023 P1 Q8 (1 mark) — students had to analyze particle motion in progressive water waves using displacement-time graphs

workedExample.question: A wave has frequency 16 Hz. From the graph, determine the speed of the wave. (3 marks)
steps: Step 1: Read the wavelength from the graph. Count the distance between two consecutive peaks or troughs. λ = 0.4 m
Step 2: Write the wave equation: v = fλ where v = speed, f = frequency, λ = wavelength
Step 3: Substitute the values: v = 16 Hz × 0.4 m = 6.4 m/s
workedExample.answer: Wavelength λ = 0.4 m (from graph), using v = fλ, speed = 16 × 0.4 = 6.4 m/s
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for correct wavelength reading, 1 mark for correct equation, 1 mark for correct calculation with unit

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused wavelength with amplitude when reading wave graphs' — always measure the distance between identical points in phase, not the maximum displacement
Examiner Report 2024: 'Students frequently stated phase difference as 90° when points were clearly half a wavelength apart' — remember that λ/2 separation always gives 180° phase difference
Examiner Report 2022: 'Candidates often forgot to include units in wave speed calculations' — always state the final answer as m/s for wave speed calculations
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw a transverse wave showing: wavelength λ measured peak-to-peak or trough-to-trough, amplitude A measured from equilibrium to peak, frequency f as oscillations per second. Include labels for compression and rarefaction on longitudinal waves, and demonstrate that particles oscillate perpendicular to wave direction (transverse) or parallel to wave direction (longitudinal).
note_data.quickRecap: Magnets have north and south poles that create magnetic fields, with like poles repelling and opposite poles attracting. Magnetic materials can be temporarily or permanently magnetised, while electromagnets use current in coiled wire around soft iron. Electric charges create forces between objects, with conductors allowing charge flow and insulators blocking it unless charged by friction.
